sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 54-year-old male, presents with a 2-3 month history of stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheek bone area of his face, which is exacerbated by cold temperatures and certain sensory stimuli.
He reports that the pain is excruciating, often reaching a severity of 10/10, and can last anywhere from a few minutes to an hour.
He has tried ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm without relief.
He has a history of occasional headaches, but no migraines or brain tumors.
He denies numbness or tingling in the affected area and has no weakness on the right side of his face.
Past medical history: NOT MENTIONED
Review of systems: NOT MENTIONED
Current medications: NOT MENTIONED
Objective:
Vital signs: NOT MENTIONED
Physical exam: The patient has a normal examination of the face, including symmetrical smile and no weakness on the right side.
He is able to follow my finger with his eyes in both directions.
He reports a little bit of numbness on the right side of his face when cotton tape is run along the side of his face.
A needle poke on the right side feels dull, while on the left side it feels sharp.
Test Results: The MRI shows a small blood vessel abutting and possibly pinching the trigeminal nerve.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has symptoms consistent with trigeminal neuralgia, a condition where nerve compression causes shooting electrical pain in the face.
Problem list: Trigeminal neuralgia; confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Tegretol 100mg twice a day for the management of trigeminal neuralgia.
We will monitor the patient's response to the medication and consider surgical options if necessary.
Genetic testing will be done to ensure the safety of the medication.
Follow-up: The patient will return in one month to check on the effectiveness of the medication and to discuss further management if necessary.
